1: minchah (H4503): 'grain offering, tribute.' From a root meaning 'to give, to present' (related to the verb manah = to apportion). In older biblical usage minchah can refer to any tribute or gift (Gen 32:14 Jacob's gift to Esau; 1 Sam 10:27). By Leviticus it has specialized to the non-animal tribute offering. Solet (H5560): fine/sifted flour (as opposed to coarser qemach). Shemen (H8081): olive oil. Levonah (H3828): frankincense — a costly aromatic resin. The minchah accompanies the animal offerings as a secondary tribute; it is also appropriate as the offering of those who cannot afford animals (Lev 5:11).
2: 'azkara (H234): 'memorial portion' — from zakar (to remember). The priest burns a representative handful of flour, oil, and all the frankincense as the 'azkara — the portion that makes the whole offering 'remembered' (zakar) before YHWH. The Latin term is pars pro toto — a part that represents the whole. The theological claim is that the burning of a portion has the full effect of burning the whole: the representational logic underlying the entire sacrificial system (and by extension all atonement by substitution).
3: qodesh qodashim (H6944 doubled): 'most holy' — the superlative formed by doubling the noun. The remainder of the minchah after the 'azkara is burned belongs to Aaron and his sons. The classification qodesh qodashim signals that priestly portions carry the highest degree of holiness — they must be eaten within the sanctuary by male priests only. The graduated holiness system (most holy / holy / clean / unclean) is the spatial-moral architecture of Leviticus.
4: challot matza and raqiqim: 'unleavened loaves' and 'unleavened wafers.' The minchah baked in an oven must be unleavened. Matza (H4682, unleavened bread) is a requirement for all grain offerings offered as isheh (fire offering to YHWH) — Lev 2:11 will explain that leaven cannot be offered. The unleavened requirement for any grain offering brought into the altar's fire reinforces that the fire-offering category is set apart from everyday baked goods.
5: al ha-machavat (machavat, H4227): 'on the flat griddle/pan.' A shallow, flat griddle produces a thin, unleavened cake. Three cooking methods are specified (oven, griddle, deep pan) because the minchah can be prepared at different domestic facilities. The flexibility of preparation method ensures that the grain offering is accessible regardless of the offerer's cooking equipment. The common requirement across all methods: fine flour, oil, and unleavened.
6: pittim pittim: 'into pieces, pieces' — intensive repetition indicating small, evenly broken fragments. Pit (H6595): a morsel/fragment. The griddle-baked grain offering is broken into pieces and oil poured over them before being brought to the priest. The breaking and oiling is a preparatory act by the offerer before the priests handle it. The repeated breaking (intensive plural) suggests thoroughness: not roughly torn but properly portioned.
7: marcheshet (H4802): 'deep pan' or 'frying pan/cauldron' — a deeper vessel producing a thicker, richer product than the griddle. Only three mentions in the OT (all in Leviticus: 2:7; 7:9; 1 Chr 23:29 variant). The deep-pan grain offering is made with fine flour and oil like the others. The diversity of cooking vessels accommodates the range of domestic food preparation in ancient Israel while maintaining consistent sacrificial requirements.
8: hiqriva (from qarav): 'to present/bring near' — the same root as qorban (v. 1:2). The grain offering is 'brought near' to the priest who then carries it to the altar. The repeated use of qarav vocabulary throughout Leviticus consistently frames sacrifice as approach, not payment: the worshiper draws near to God through the medium of the offering.
9: The priest takes the 'azkara portion from the grain offering and burns it on the altar — reah nihoah la-YHWH (pleasing aroma to YHWH). The memorial-portion logic applies to all forms of the minchah regardless of preparation method: a representative portion goes up in smoke, and the rest goes to the priests. The 'azkara is always wholly burned; it is the part that enacts the full transaction before YHWH.
10: The remainder (ha-noteret) of the grain offering belongs to Aaron and his sons — again classified as qodesh qodashim (most holy), echoing v. 3. The double classification (vv. 3 and 10) bookends the section on preparation variants, establishing that regardless of cooking method, the priestly remainder carries the same high holiness grade and the same consumption requirements.
11: chametz (H2557): 'leaven, leavened bread' — from a root meaning 'to ferment.' Devash (H1706): 'honey' (or fruit syrup/date-honey). Both leaven and honey are forbidden from any grain offering brought as a fire offering (isheh) to YHWH. The reasons are not stated explicitly; the probable theological intuition is that fermentation and sweetening represent processes of transformation and corruption that are inappropriate for what is presented as pure tribute. In Israel's broader religious world, leaven and honey were used in Canaanite sacrificial contexts, which may inform their exclusion from Israelite altar practice.
12: bikkurim (H1061): 'firstfruits' — from bakhar (to be early, to be first). The first produce of the harvest can be brought to YHWH as an offering (qorban), but the firstfruits are not to be offered on the altar as a reah nihoah. This differentiates the grain-offering system (which can generate reah nihoah via the 'azkara) from the firstfruits offering, which is presented but not burned as incense. Lev 23:9-14 will specify the firstfruits wave-offering in detail.
13: melach ha-berit Elohekha (melach, H4417): 'the salt of the covenant of your God.' Salt is a required addition to every grain offering: 'do not omit the salt of the covenant.' Salt preserves and prevents corruption; in ancient Near Eastern contexts, salt-covenant language denotes the permanence and trustworthiness of a treaty or agreement. Berit melach ('covenant of salt') appears also in Num 18:19 (the priestly covenant) and 2 Chr 13:5 (the Davidic covenant) — a perpetual, incorruptible commitment. Every grain offering seasoned with salt is a small enactment of the covenant's permanent character.
14: 'omer geresh (geresh, H1643): 'crushed/beaten grain.' Aviv (H24): 'fresh/young grain ears' — in early spring, before full ripening. The firstfruits grain offering uses aviv (the name of the first month, Chodesh ha-Aviv, is derived from this term). The grain is roasted (qalui ba-esh) and then beaten into crushed pieces before being brought. The firstfruits offering thus uses grain at its earliest stage — the beginning of the harvest — as representative of the whole yield to come.
15: venatata aleiha shemen vesamt aleiha levonah: oil poured on and frankincense placed on the firstfruits grain offering — making it a proper minchah in form. The combination of crushed grain, oil, and frankincense is the standard minchah triad (as in v. 1). The firstfruits offering, despite its distinct occasion (harvest-beginning), uses the same sacred vocabulary of materials as the regular minchah.
16: The priest burns the 'azkara (memorial portion) — some of the geresh (crushed grain) with some of the oil and all the frankincense — as a reah nihoah la-YHWH. The 'azkara pattern closes the grain-offering chapter as it opened it (v. 2): whatever the variety, form, or occasion of the minchah, the memorial-portion logic applies. A part, properly burned, makes the whole acceptable.
